summarization.a: The figure illustrates a reaction pathway with various transition states (TS) and intermediates (Im) along with their corresponding Gibbs free energy changes (ΔG<sub>298K</sub>) at 298 Kelvin for the O<sub>2</sub> plasma half-reaction.

a.question: What are the different oxygen radicals/states present in the oxygen half-reaction?
Compare the energy profiles of oxygen radicals and conclude which is the most stable state
a.answer:
The energy of the excited state of the oxygen molecule, <sup>1</sup>O<sub>2</sub>, is 38.3 kcal/mol higher than that of the ground state. 
The energy of two oxygen radicals, 2(<sup>3</sup>O) , is 74.6 kcal/mol higher than that of <sup>1</sup>O<sub>2</sub> . The energy of  the excited states of two oxygen radicals, 2(<sup>1</sup>O), is at the highest amounting to 236.4 kcal/ mol. The ground state of the oxygen molecule, <sup>3</sup>O<sub>2</sub> at 0, is the most stable.

a.question: Does the ground state of the oxygen molecule form only one intermediate product?
Are other oxygen radicals active? Which radicals has more than one intermediates?
a.answer: The other oxygen radicals are active species and can oxidize the surface silane to form reactions (B2–B5). With <sup>1</sup>O<sub>2</sub>  there are two pathways (B2 and B3) for oxidation of the silane.
